summary: A 13-lesson course that equips an intermediate Python developer to explain, justify, and demonstrate every production-quality scripting best practice — from toolchain automation to concurrency — using 22 official sources as the evidence base.

modules.title: The Python Quality Toolchain
modules.strata_theme: Theme 1: Automate Style Enforcement; Theme 3: pyproject.toml as Single Config Hub
modules.description: How to configure ruff, mypy, and pytest in one place so correctness is enforced automatically rather than by discipline
objectives: Explain why automated enforcement is more reliable than code review for style and type safety
Illustrate the role each tool (ruff, mypy, pytest) plays in the quality stack
Justify using pyproject.toml as the single configuration file for all dev tools

lessons.title: Automating Quality: ruff, mypy, and pytest
lessons.description: Why the three-tool quality stack (ruff, mypy, pytest) is non-negotiable, how each tool fills a unique gap, and how to configure all three in pyproject.toml

lessons.title: pyproject.toml: One File to Configure Them All
lessons.description: How pyproject.toml serves as both the dev-tool configuration hub and the runtime config source (via tomllib), and why this reduces cognitive overhead

modules.title: Script Structure and Observability
modules.strata_theme: Theme 6: Standard Script Structure; Theme 4: Structured Logging
modules.description: The universal main()-based skeleton that makes scripts testable and auditable, and how to route all observability through the logging hierarchy
objectives: Explain the execution sequence of a well-structured Python script and why order matters
Compare print()-based debugging with structured logging and justify the difference to a junior developer
Illustrate how argparse, logging, and main() work together as a single coherent system

lessons.title: The Universal Script Skeleton
lessons.description: The if __name__ == '__main__' guard, the main() execution sequence (parse → log → validate → dispatch), and why this pattern makes scripts testable and importable

lessons.title: Structured Logging Over print()
lessons.description: Module-level loggers, logging.basicConfig() in main(), the library/script distinction, and how to expose --log-level as a CLI argument

modules.title: Documentation and the Type System
modules.strata_theme: Theme 2: Type Annotations Are the Default for New Code
modules.description: How PEP 257 docstrings and Python 3.10+ type annotations together form a machine-verifiable and human-readable function contract
objectives: Explain the complementary roles of docstrings and type annotations in making code self-documenting
Illustrate correct usage of Python 3.10+ annotation syntax (str | None, list[T], TypedDict, Protocol, Final)
Compare the Google Style Guide's 'mandatory annotations' position against PEP 484's 'never mandatory' framing and explain the resolution to a team

lessons.title: Writing Self-Documenting Functions
lessons.description: PEP 257 Google-style docstrings (summary + Args/Returns/Raises), type annotation requirements for public functions in new code, and how ruff enforces both

lessons.title: Python 3.10+ Type System: Union, TypedDict, Protocol, Final
lessons.description: The modern annotation vocabulary — str | None, list[T], TypedDict for dict structures, Protocol for structural typing, Final for constants — and when to use each

modules.title: Resource Management and Configuration
modules.strata_theme: Theme 5: Context Managers and pathlib; Theme 10: Secrets in Env Vars or Files
modules.description: How pathlib replaces os.path, why context managers are mandatory for all resources, and how to layer configuration safely without secrets in source
objectives: Explain why pathlib.Path is preferred over os.path and demonstrate at least three API improvements
Justify the use of context managers for every resource lifecycle, including temporary files
Compare TOML, INI, and environment variables as configuration sources and explain when to use each

lessons.title: pathlib and Context Managers: Safe I/O by Default
lessons.description: pathlib.Path replacing os.path, the with-statement requirement for all resources, and why TemporaryDirectory is safer than mktemp()

lessons.title: Configuration and Secrets: Files, Env Vars, Never Source
lessons.description: The layered config priority (CLI > env > file > default), tomllib for TOML, configparser for INI, and os.environ.get() for secrets

modules.title: Data Serialization
modules.description: How to choose the right serialization format by trust level — json and csv for external data, pickle only for internal trusted caches
objectives: Explain the security risk of deserializing untrusted pickle data and illustrate the correct policy to a team
Compare json, csv, TOML, and pickle by use case and justify the correct choice for a given scenario

lessons.title: Choosing the Right Serialization Format
lessons.description: The format-to-trust-level mapping: json/csv for external data, TOML for developer config, pickle only for internal trusted caches — and why this matters for security

modules.title: Concurrency and Graceful Shutdown
modules.strata_theme: Theme 7: Concurrency Model by Task Type; Theme 8: Graceful Shutdown First-Class
modules.description: How to choose between asyncio, ThreadPoolExecutor, and ProcessPoolExecutor, and how to structure shutdown so long-running scripts exit cleanly under any condition
objectives: Explain the decision matrix for concurrency model selection (IO-bound vs CPU-bound, sync vs async libraries)
Compare asyncio.gather() and asyncio.TaskGroup and justify which to use on Python 3.11+
Illustrate a complete graceful shutdown pattern spanning signal handlers, CancelledError, and executor context managers

lessons.title: asyncio, Threads, or Processes: The Decision Matrix
lessons.description: Task-type-based selection (IO-bound → asyncio or threads, CPU-bound → ProcessPoolExecutor), asyncio.to_thread() as the bridge, and TaskGroup vs gather() for Python 3.11+

lessons.title: Graceful Shutdown: SIGTERM, CancelledError, and Cleanup
lessons.description: Registering SIGTERM/SIGINT handlers, handling CancelledError in finally blocks in asyncio tasks, and letting executor with-blocks drain pending work

modules.title: Teaching Complications and Honest Limits
modules.description: The genuine debates and open questions in this field — how to present them honestly when teaching, and what to do when evidence runs out
objectives: Compare the PEP 484 'never mandatory' and Google 'mandatory for all new code' positions and explain the resolution to a team without dismissing either
Explain the three open gaps in this research (dataclasses, packaging, pre-commit) and what learners should read next

lessons.title: The Type Annotation Debate: Helping Your Team Decide
lessons.description: PEP 484 vs Google Style Guide on annotation enforcement — the historical context, the real resolution, and how to present both sides fairly to a team adopting types incrementally

lessons.title: Gaps and Follow-Ups: What This Research Left Open
lessons.description: The four open gaps — dataclasses, packaging/distribution, dependency management, pre-commit hooks — why they're out of scope, and where to go next

learning_plan.notes: Each lesson targets ≤800 words / ~10–12 min reading time. One lesson per 15-min session. Milestones mark the end of each major block: toolchain (L01), documentation/types (L05), concurrency (L10).
